data.title: CubeSmart Is A Strong Buy
data.description:
I recently shared an article that explained how self-storage REITs could become one of the biggest hidden winners of the AI revolution.

That’s because it is expected to lead to significant labor market disruption, which will then lead to greater mobility as people look for new jobs, creating lots of new demand for self-storage.

Moreover, AI is also making it much easier than ever before to start all sorts of new businesses, and therefore, I expect it to lead to a boom in new small business formation. These new entrepreneurs will not need large industrial warehouses. Rather, they will need a small and flexible self-storage unit for their storage needs.

Finally, because self-storage is one of the most operationally intensive property sectors, it should also benefit a lot from new efficiencies unlocked thanks to AI, potentially leading to higher margins.

All of this has the potential to greatly boost the cash flows of self-storage REITs over the coming five years, and yet, the market does not seem to have yet recognized this long-term opportunity, as most investors remain focused on the near-term challenges caused by oversupply.

It has caused self-storage REITs Public Storage (PSA), Extra Space Storage (EXR), and CubeSmart (CUBE) to crash in recent years, pushing their valuations to historic lows:

I think that this is a great buying opportunity, as these REITs could present significant upside potential as growth accelerates and the narratives shift to how this sector is a hidden winner of AI.

But which self-storage REIT is the best opportunity today?

  • Public Storage?
  • Extra Space Storage?
  • CubeSmart?
  • Or even National Storage Affiliates (NSA)?

I think that it is CUBE. Here are five reasons why:

Reason No. 1: Best Internal Growth Prospects

Its assets enjoy the greatest barriers to entry. They're typically located in densely populated areas, such as Brooklyn, which will limit the long-term growth of new supply and give its assets greater pricing power as the new demand from AI kicks in:

Here's a comparison of all four self-storage REITs:

PSA and EXR also own good assets, but they're far more exposed to future supply risk.

NSA is far behind in terms of asset quality, focusing more on secondary and tertiary markets.

Reason No. 2: Best Long-Term External Growth Prospects

CUBE also stands out in its external growth prospects in two ways.

First, it has a rapidly growing property management business, which does not rely on its own capital. It simply offers management services to other self-storage landlords in exchange for a cut of the profits. It allows it to keep growing even when its own capital is too expensive. It also provides it with a large pipeline of future potential acquisition opportunities.

Second, its size is in the sweet spot of being large enough to enjoy significant economies of scale but not too big, which could slow down its growth.

I fear that PSA and EXR are getting too big for their own good with market caps in excess of $30 billion, making it harder for them to identify enough new investment opportunities that really move the needle.

Market Cap
Public Storage (PSA) $54 billion
Extra Space Storage (EXR) $32 billion
CubeSmart (CUBE) $9 billion

NSA is even smaller than CUBE and could enjoy strong periods of external growth prospects, but its inferior average asset quality will likely lead to greater volatility in its performance and market sentiment, making its access to capital markets less predictable. Moreover, it's now getting bought out by Public Storage anyway, likely ending its chapter as an independent company.

Reason No. 3: Lowest Valuation

Despite owning better assets on average and enjoying better long-term growth prospects, CUBE is quite a bit cheaper than its larger peers, EXR and PSA:

Following its recent surge, NSA is also now trading at a higher P/FFO multiple, despite owning much weaker assets and having quite a bit more leverage:

Reason No. 4: Highest Safe Dividend Yield

As a result of its lower valuation, CUBE is also offering a materially higher dividend yield than EXR and PSA:

NSA yields as much, despite its much higher payout ratio, greater leverage, and lower-quality (higher cap rate) assets.

I have previously predicted that NSA is likely to cut its dividend in 2026 as a result of its high leverage and poor dividend coverage. This will now happen as a result of the all-stock deal, merging with Public Storage, and reducing the dividend of former NSA investors.

CUBE, on the other hand, is safely covering its dividend and just recently even hiked it - a clear signal that its dividend is safe.

Bottom Line

Active REIT investors seeking to maximize total returns may want to favor CUBE.

That does not mean that PSA, EXR, or even NSA will do poorly over time.

It simply means that CUBE is likely to outperform its peer group going forward, and this is why we invest in it at High Yield Landlord.

data.title: Toast: More Than Just A POS
data.description:
Many investors see Toast (TOST) as ‘just’ a restaurant Point of Sale provider. However, in recent years the company has broadened its horizons considerably, and can now be viewed as something of a combined POS, fintech, and hardware provider. Moreover, the company is scaling rapidly in terms of new locations, gross payment volume, and ARR. At a reasonable valuation compared to its main listed peer Block (XYZ), and plenty of cash on the balance sheet to fuel growth, the company looks compelling.

Growth Is Broadening

The company’s Q4 and FY 2025 report was jam-packed with solid growth figures, not only in existing markets, but new ones as well. Overall revenue was up 24% for the year driven by a 23% increase in GPV (gross payment volume) and a 26% increase in ARR (annualized recurring run-rate). These are key metrics for what this company is increasingly becoming, namely a combined platform that integrates POS, inventory management and payments. An example of this broader strategy is the recent strategic partnership with Instacart, which will create a “unified local shelf” for businesses to synchronize their physical inventory with the Instacart marketplace.

Free cash flow was especially impressive coming in at $608 million for the year, which means that they converted nearly 10% of their $6.153 billion in revenue and nearly twice their GAAP net income of $342 million into cash. Moreover, GAAP net income was up from $19 million in the previous year for an increase of around 18 times.

Another impressive number was the record 30,000 new net locations, which includes international growth. With operations in Canada, the UK, and Ireland, the company entered Australia as its fourth international market and expects to keep expanding into other countries selectively where they have a “right to compete and win.”

For Q1, analysts are expecting non-GAAP EPS of $0.27, up from $0.20 in the year earlier period for a healthy 35% year-over-year increase but flat sequentially. Management has guided to adjusted EBITDA of $160 to $170 million, while non-GAAP subscription services and financial technology solutions gross profit is expected to rise by 22-24% compared to Q1 2025.

The Bread And Butter

One of Toast’s key strengths is its increasing vertical integration across different revenue streams which now encompasses POS, payments, inventory, payroll, loyalty, and ordering. As such, it is trying to deeply embed itself in the daily operations of restaurants and hospitality businesses, which would increase cross-sell and upsell potential as well as raise switching costs. This is essentially its moat; the fact that customers like the platform, the UI, and the integrated workflows. Management is also investing in AI tools through ToastIQ, which could improve customer retention and raise software monetization over time.

These benefits are also reflected in new partnerships, for example with Papa Murphy’s, which will add 1,000 locations in the US. According to management, owner MTY Food Group likes Toast “for its modern, flexible platform that can support multiple operating models on one system, along with the flexible product features needed for a growing pizza chain”.

Typically, a company at this stage of the growth curve would be reinvesting heavily to accelerate momentum, but Toast is already translating scale into cash, which we can see in free cash flow generation. It currently has more than $2 billion in cash and marketable securities that it can use to fuel further growth, buy back shares as announced, and even fund M&A in the form of tuck-ins or a full strategic acquisition. Management has not said much about M&A, so this remains speculative at this point.

Valuation Looks Reasonable

The company currently trades at a FWD Non-GAAP P/E of 22.67, which is higher than Block’s 20.5, but not overly so. It’s also not much more expensive on revenue-based metrics, with Toast at 2.76x versus Block’s 1.87x. The premium can be explained through the higher growth Toast enjoys, with a 5-year revenue CAGR of 49.5% versus Block’s 20.5%. Where Toast doesn’t look as attractive is FWD EV/EBITDA at 18.8x, a clear premium to Block’s 9.8 and nearly double the sector median of 9.7.

As management has indicated, profitability is not yet mature as the company continues to invest in top line expansion. As such, the company’s gross margins are not in line with Block yet. Block has a superior gross margin of 43% versus Toast’s 26%, which is partly due to the companies’ different revenue mix. However, they’re almost equal on net income margin around 5.5%. This is encouraging given the fact that Toast has not switched to full monetization mode yet.

Risks & Catalysts

The company remains heavily exposed to the restaurant and hospitality sector, which can be sensitive to slower consumer spending especially with inflation being sticky, as well as wage inflation and failure rates among smaller retail operators. Another risk is continued competition in the space, as Block, Clover, and legacy providers all compete for market share which may result in pricing pressure.

On the catalyst side, continued 20%+ ARR growth, strong net location additions, and further free cash flow expansion could support upside. Additional restaurant chain adds, successful international launches, and accelerated share repurchases backed by its $2 billion liquidity position could also improve sentiment.

Conclusion

Toast occupies an interesting niche in the vertical SaaS sector, as it combines POS, payments, inventory management, payroll and other services in one package. Growth remains strong on the basis of overall revenue, GPV, new locations, and ARR, and the company is still scaling. Meanwhile, roughly $2 billion in cash and marketable securities gives Toast plenty of opportunities for expansion, buybacks, and reinvestment. Valuation doesn’t look cheap on every metric, but it looks reasonable for the kind of growth that the company is delivering. With an attractive mix of growth, earnings quality, and balance sheet strength, I rate Toast a Buy.

data.title: Dollar Reasserts Itself As Global Tensions Shift Currency Markets
data.description:
By Jim Iuorio

For over a year, the U.S. dollar has been under sustained pressure. Then, in the first week of March, it suddenly found its footing, gaining over 2.5% against both the euro and the yen, and then, by March 16, 3% against the euro. The question isn't whether the dollar rallied, but rather how sustainable the move is and what it means for the broader currency landscape heading into the back half of 2026.

The catalyst was geopolitical. On Saturday, February 28, the United States launched a military offensive on Iran. Markets responded with sharp volatility across asset classes, but the most telling moves came in foreign exchange. The dollar, which had been steadily losing ground, abruptly reversed course. What followed was a chain reaction through energy markets, inflation expectations, Fed policy pricing and ultimately, currency positioning across the globe.

The Setup: A Year of Dollar Weakness

To understand the reversal, it helps to step back and look at the last two years. The euro had surged from $1.02 in early 2025 to $1.21 by January 2026 — an 18% gain driven by expectations of aggressive Fed easing, concerns about U.S. trade policy pushing capital away from American assets and the unwinding of a massive long-dollar positioning from late 2024. The dollar was under sustained selling pressure, and markets were positioning for continued weakness.

Then oil spiked, and everything changed.

Oil, Inflation and the Fed Reassessment

Conflict in the Middle East triggered a sharp increase in oil prices. WTI crude oil futures rose from roughly $65 per barrel to over $119 by the March 9 trading session — an 84% gain in just over a week. That kind of upward volatility has implications far beyond the pump.

Two-year inflation breakevens spiked from 2.8% to 3.2% as markets reassessed the inflation outlook. More critically, expectations for Fed easing plummeted. Prior to the conflict, the CME FedWatch tool showed the market pricing roughly 50 basis points of rate cuts by year-end 2026. That number decreased to approximately 0 basis points of cuts by March 23.

The thesis behind this shift seems fairly straightforward: the oil spike was inflationary, and the Fed might not have the runway to ease into a softening labor market without risking a resurgence in price pressures. Higher U.S. rates, or at least the expectation of less accommodation, are a structural tailwind for the dollar. While German yields also moved higher, the differential between U.S. policy rates and those of other major economies widened materially. That's a key fundamental driver behind the dollar's recent strength.

So was this a safe-haven bid? The move doesn’t appear to be purely about safe-haven flows. The dollar's 2.5% gain against both the euro and the yen was significant but not necessarily strong enough to suggest pure panic buying. If safe-haven demand were the dominant force, one might expect a more explosive reaction, particularly from the yen.

Yet, there were signs of heightened risk aversion — Euro currency CVOL, a CME Group index that measures implied volatility in the euro, spiked over 30% after the conflict. While this was a substantial move, it came off of an 18-month low for the index, suggesting measured concern rather than an indication of panic.

The better explanation is a combination: some flight-to-quality demand, layered on top of a rate differential story that was already starting to favor the U.S.

This interpretation is supported by the CME Commitment of Traders (COT) tool, which showed corresponding institutional liquidation of long Euro positions that had been established during the previous period of Euro strength.

What About Other Currencies?

The broader dollar story is playing out unevenly across different currency pairs.

The yen's position is particularly precarious. Japan imports virtually all of its energy, so sustained high crude prices hit its terms of trade directly. The Bank of Japan has kept rates near zero while other central banks have tightened, leaving the yen vulnerable to widening rate differential.

Commodity-linked currencies like the Australian and Canadian dollar have seen some support from higher oil prices, but that's been offset by risk-off sentiment and concerns about global growth. The Aussie is particularly sensitive to Chinese demand, and with China's economy still navigating structural headwinds, AUD hasn't been able to capitalize on the commodity rally.

Emerging market (EM) currencies are another story. Higher U.S. rates and a stronger dollar create pressure for EM central banks, many of which are dealing with their own inflation challenges. If the dollar continues to strengthen, we could see renewed stress in countries with dollar-denominated debt or trade imbalances.

The British pound has been relatively resilient, trading in a narrow range against the dollar. The Bank of England has maintained a cautious stance, and while UK inflation has moderated, it hasn't collapsed to the degree that would force aggressive easing. The pound’s near-term path will likely track the euro-dollar — if the euro weakens materially, the pound probably follows, though to a lesser degree.

The Path Forward

The dollar's trajectory over the rest of 2026 will likely be shaped by three forces: monetary policy divergence, geopolitical risk and U.S. trade policy. Right now, the first two favor the dollar, while the third cuts against it. Below are three potential scenarios to consider:

  • Scenario 1: Sustained Tension and Dollar Strength - If tensions remain elevated and oil stays above $100, the Fed's limited easing path and safe-haven flows support continued dollar strength.
  • Scenario 2: Stabilization and Range-Bound Trading - If geopolitical risk ebbs and oil stabilizes, the dollar likely consolidates recent gains in a range-bound pattern. Market participants would reassess Fed policy expectations based on incoming economic data.
  • Scenario 3: Resolution and Renewed Weakness - A quick resolution to Middle East tensions coupled with deteriorating U.S. economic data could push the Fed back toward aggressive easing and renewed dollar weakness.

Positioning matters too. The massive build-up of euro longs during 2025's rally hasn't entirely unwound. If safe-haven buying continues and those positions get squeezed, euro liquidation could accelerate the dollar’s move lower. From a technical perspective, the euro-dollar is testing key support around $1.15, with a break below opening the door to $1.10. For dollar-yen, the ¥150 level is psychologically important.

Trading The Move

For those looking to hedge currency exposure or express a directional view, CME Group offers efficient tools across multiple pairs. Euro FX futures provide meaningful capital efficiency at nearly 50-to-1 leverage, while Micro contracts (one-tenth the size) allow for tighter risk management. Similar Micro contracts exist for the yen, pound and other major currencies.

The most prudent posture for traders is cautious and nimble. The landscape can shift quickly, and proper position sizing matters more than usual in this environment. The dollar is having a moment — whether it's a short-term bounce or the beginning of a sustained reversal will depend on how the next few months unfold.

Original Post

data.title: UFP Technologies: Great Growth At Just An Okay Price
data.description:
When looking for new and interesting companies to analyze, one firm that came across my radar was UFP Technologies (UFPT). This is an intriguing business, serving as a developer and producer of single use and single patient medical devices. This is a massive industry as you will see shortly. And it is one that the business has done quite well in over the last few years. Revenue, profits, and cash clothes, have all risen nicely. And relative to other similar firms, the stock is trading at levels that are quite attractive. The only reason, honestly, why I cannot turn bullish on the business right now is because, on an absolute basis, the stock looks more fairly valued than it does undervalued. But it wouldn't take much for me to change my opinion here. If we see the share price drop another 10%, or if we see rapid growth throughout this year, that could easily bridge the gap. But for now, I think that calling it a 'hold' candidate makes the most sense here.

A great business at just an okay price

As I mentioned already, UFP Technologies operates as a producer of single use and single patient medical devices. I know this is kind of amorphous. So it would be helpful to dig more into precisely what it does. For instance, the company produces custom medical products such as protective drapes for robotic surgeries, patient handling and comfort, advanced wound care, infection prevention, disposables that are used in surgical and endoscopic procedures, and even packaging for medical devices and orthopedic implants. There are other examples as well such as cardiac implant components, dispenser coils used in the production of catheters, and more. But you get the idea.

Management has made clear that this is a massive space to play in. The MedTech industry as the company calls it is worth about $550 billion globally. This industry includes the global medical device CDMO market that is worth about $101 billion and that's growing at an annualized rate of about 10%. But as a whole, the MedTech industry is growing at a rate of about 6% annually. Perhaps the most exciting category in this market would be the surfaces and support industry that is responsible for about 23% of the company's medical sales. It's growing at a low double digit rate. But there are other components of the space that are growing attractively also. This includes the infection prevention market, the orthopedics market, the therapeutics market, the wound care market, and the diagnostics market, all of which are growing at a mid single digit rate. The robotic surgery industry that is responsible for about 29% of medical sales is growing at a mid teens rate. So clearly, the company has a lot going for it.

When it comes specifically to the robotic surgery industry, this is a market that, over the next several years, should do exceptionally well. In 2024, it was worth approximately $11 billion. But it should more than doubled to $23.7 billion by 2029. This is in spite of the fact that we already have a large number of surgeries being conducted by this means already. There are more than 300 million major surgeries performed annually using robotics. And even when you look at the soft surgery market, that number is a solid 44 million. As the leading provider of several materials and capabilities that are necessary for this space, UFP Technologies stands to benefit from continued expansion. And of course, the firm is targeting other opportunities as well. This includes not only expanding its business with existing customers, such as through the addition of new value added services, but through additional innovation that increases its own capabilities.

Management is also not afraid to invest in acquisition activities. In July of last year, for instance, the company acquired Techno Plastics Industries, which operates as a specialty producer of precision thermoplastic injection molded components. That was a rather small purchase, costing the company about $4.3 million. But also at around the same time, the company purchased Universal Plastics & Engineering Company, a producer of precision thermoformed and heat sealed polymer components that are used for shielding batteries and certain implantable medical devices. That ended up costing about $7.6 million. There are other acquisitions as well, but you get the idea.

Management has taken advantage of the opportunities presented by this growing market to expand nicely over the last few years. From 2023 through 2025, revenue jumped from $400.1 million to $602.8 million. Most of that growth seems to have been the result of acquisitions that the company made over the last couple of years. In fact, for 2025, companies acquired in 2024 and 2025 brought in $168.3 million of revenue. This is not to say that the firm has not been capable of achieving organic growth. Organic revenue actually increased by 1.5% in 2025.

As revenue for the company has expanded, profitability has increased also. Over the last three years, net profits surged from $44.9 million to $68.3 million. But that's not all. Adjusted net income grew from $51 million to $76.1 million. Operating cash flow more than doubled from $41.3 million to $91.9 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $65.5 million to $101.6 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $77.2 million to $121.1 million.

From what I can tell, management has not provided comprehensive guidance for 2026. However, they have expressed that further growth is on the horizon. This should come in part from new program launches, including those in the patient services market that involve infection prevention and robotic surgery activities. Those are expected to be the primary drivers of organic expansion for the year. The firm is not focused only on growth. It is also working on reducing overall expenses. One example would be operating inefficiencies at its AJR facility. Management mentioned in their annual report what they refer to as the 'AJR Labor Issue'. After the company acquired AJR Enterprises in 2024, it conducted a review of the legal work eligibility of employees there. The end result here was employee turnover of more than 50% of the workforce there. This then required the company to hire and train replacement workers that ended up negatively impacting productivity. But the good news is that management expects those troubles to be drawing to a close. For instance, they highlighted in their latest earnings call that they have made progress in that regard and they expect the impact of inefficiencies to diminish even more in the first quarter of this year and beyond. So that is excellent to see.

Given how the company has performed in recent years, it's awfully tempting to be bullish about it. I certainly am from an operational standpoint. But, from a valuation standpoint, I cannot pull the trigger on a bullish assessment. In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using results from 2024 and 2025. These certainly aren't lofty valuations. But they do fit into the fair value category as I typically describe it. Relative to other similar firms, it is worth noting that the stock is a bit on the cheap end of the spectrum. In that same chart, you can see that only one company ended up being cheaper than it is on a price to earnings basis. One was cheaper and another was tied with it on a price to operating cash flow basis. And then, on an EV to EBITDA basis, two firms ended up being cheaper.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I must say that I am optimistic from an operational standpoint when it comes to UFP Technologies. But I cannot be bullish on it from a valuation standpoint. Yes, the company is attractively priced compared to other similar firms. But relative valuation only goes so far with me. I like opportunities that are basically a guaranteed bet or that are so discounted that a lot would have to go wrong in order for me to underperform the market. And even though I expect the business to continue expanding for the foreseeable future, I just don't see that kind of situation here. Because of that, I think that rating it a 'hold' is the right choice right now.

data.title: Adobe: One Of The Cheapest Franchises In Software
data.description:
Introduction

Adobe (ADBE) has been one of the most punished software stock starting the year with the rise of AI tools as it lost nearly 27% YTD and around 60% of its all-time high, reducing its overall market cap of $310B to now $100B. This is based on the narrative that models are commoditizing image generation and making Photoshop irrelevant, while competitors such as Canva are eating away at Creative Cloud. I’ll dive into why I believe Adobe still represents an opportunity in today’s market.

Current Dynamics

I first want to dive into the latest earnings as they capture the current disconnect between the perceived value of Adobe with what has been really happening on the market. Revenue came out to $6.4B during fiscal Q1, which is actually up 12% Y/Y, beating the street estimates by around $120MM. Subscription revenue itself grew by 12%, while business professionals & consumers are at $1.78B, a 16% increase. Non-GAAP EPS reached $6.06, beating the consensus of $5.86 meanwhile, operating cash flow set a record at $2.96B, up 19% Y/Y. Looking ahead, management has guided for a full-year revenue of between $25.9 and $26.1B and up to $23.5 in non-GAAP EPS meaning that the company is definitely beating all doom expectations.

What I want to say is that, simply, these are not the numbers of a company facing an imminent decline. The business has continued to grow its revenue in the AI age at around 10.75% CAGR over the past 3 years, while at the same time expanding its FCF margins. Basically, the valuation got ahead of itself and the firm has been paying for it, though it now boasts the smallest P/E in its recent history at 14.85.

Adobe has also gone through a pivot that has been underappreciated, as the firm is now targeting agentic orchestration, meaning that Adobe has repositioned its entire Creative Cloud suite as an operating system for agents instead of focusing on singular features such as Generative Fill or text-to-image. For example, the Firefly AI assistant maintains persistent memory across sessions and can execute over 100 pre-built creative skills such as batch-editing lighting across an entire video timeline in Premiere Pro.

This is basically a strong defensive response to the effective commoditization of image generation as it integrates AI at the orchestration layer, preserving Adobe’s pixel-perfect moat. It also allows professional creators to maintain a streak between conversational AI guidance and manual and high-fidelity editing. This all ensures that Adobe remains the professional standard.

The collaboration with Anthropic (ANTHRO) is also a strong, pivotal strategic move within this agentic era, as Adobe will integrate Firefly AI assistant directly into Anthropic’s Claude chatbot, Adobe is meeting creators where they ideate. This open ecosystem approach, which now extends with OpenAI, Google Cloud and Microsoft, is basically laying the foundation for the future of software interaction through AI-interfaces.

Adobe has also decided to acquire Semrush for $1.9B in a push into the Digital Experience sector’s new frontier, being generative engine optimization. Semrush will provide Adobe with the tracking and monitoring capabilities necessary to manage brands' visibility inside these answer engines. Basically, it provides tools to ensure that a company’s website is machine-recommendable which is mission-critical for 99% of the firm’s customers.

Valuation

As I mentioned earlier, Adobe is now trading at some of its lowest multiples in its recent history, with a P/E Non-GAAP of 11.27, drastically lower than any of its peers, such as Microsoft (MSFT) at 27.58 or even Salesforce (CRM) at 14.91. The same trend could be seen with the firm’s EV/EBITDA at 10.48 and what is even more astonishing would be the firm's free cash flow generation that now translates to a yield of around 10%, which is simply astonishing in today’s software market. The price is also the lowest we have seen since 2018, when the firm was only generating $9B in revenue. I also did a scenario analysis of the firm and a DCF to also grasp the undervaluation of shares at the current prices.

On a scenario-based view, even my most bearish thesis would imply a share price of $258, an actual upside from today’s prices. On a probability weighted target, the share price would reach a strong $422, or around a 65.5% upside from here. On a DCF model base, the implied share price is closer to $312, based on a high WACC of 10.6% and a terminal growth of 2%, implying an upside of 26.4%.

I’m the most bullish on the launch of the Firefly AI assistant during the month that is basically one of the first creative agents capable of orchestrating multi-step tasks across the entire Creative Cloud suite from a single conversational interface. Adobe is also expanding into the Experience Cloud with Adobe Brand Concierge, which is an AI-powered conversational application that turns static websites into dynamic and personalized shopping experiences. With apparently shoppers who arrive at retail sites via AI chatbots are 38% more likely to make a purchase as there are more intent to buy, while Bain is estimating that the US agentic commerce market could reach $300B to $500B by 2030, representing up to 25% of all e-commerce.

Furthermore, despite the doom and gloom of AI on Adobe’s business, margins have effectively improved since the launch of AI as gross margin went from 87.7% to 89.4%, while net went from 27% to 29.5%. More so, free cash flow margin, which dipped to 35.8% back in 2023, has now reached 42.4%, which is simply enormous for a company of that size. This all allows Adobe to have great return metrics, such as an astonishing ROE of 62.5%, while ROCE is also great at 48.9%.

Risks

There is a leadership transition risk as the departure of longtime CEO Shantanu Narayen caused the stock to drop. The market saw the loss of his steady hand who navigated the company’s historic cloud pivot as a negative and a botched succession process or a radical shift in strategy under an outsider hire could continue to scare the market.

The legacy high-margin stock photography business is eroding faster than management anticipated due to the rise of gen AI alternatives. In the company’s Q1, this decline was responsible for a $70MM drag on net new ARR and if the erosion of these legacy assets continues to outpace the monetization of new AI tools, it will definitely suppress net new ARR for several more quarters.

Conclusion

I believe that the overall SaaS sector selloff has been overblown and has hit Adobe the hardest, while it is one of the most enterprise-entrenched software companies out there. The company has recorded record cash flows and a super aggressive $25B buyback strategy that signals that Adobe is through the worst of the transition volatility. Though the leadership transition and the stock business drag remain near-term hurdles, the underlying engine that is Creative Cloud is effectively accelerating into the AI era. I rate Adobe as a Strong Buy at these prices.

data.title: French Business Confidence Confirms Weakness In Domestic Demand
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier, Senior Economist, France and Switzerland

Deteriorating growth outlook

As expected, business confidence in France deteriorated sharply in April, down three points over the month. The decline is particularly pronounced in retail trade, where the indicator fell by six points, as well as in services. In both sectors, the overall outlook has darkened significantly: expected demand is declining, and order intentions have contracted sharply.

By contrast, business confidence remained stable in construction and improved slightly in industry. In this sector, overall order books are recovering, as are firms’ own production expectations. A similar pattern is observed in the PMI indicators, with the services PMI deteriorating sharply, while the manufacturing PMI improved.

The increased vulnerability of Asian industrial competitors to the current energy crisis (due to their heavy reliance on supplies transiting through the Strait of Hormuz) appears to be supporting the competitiveness of French firms. In addition, the risk of shortages and price rises seems to be prompting industrial clients to bring forward their orders. This is a clearly positive signal for French industrial conditions. However, its durability remains uncertain and is closely tied to developments in the geopolitical situation in the Strait of Hormuz.

This support for industry will help the French economy limit the immediate risk of slipping into recession. However, it will not be sufficient to offset the deterioration in domestic demand, which is very visible in the April data. Rising energy prices appear to be weighing heavily on household purchasing behaviour. They are curbing consumption, and this is directly weakening activity in retail trade and services. This weakness in domestic demand will significantly constrain economic growth this year. We now forecast growth of 0.7% in France in 2026, compared with 1% expected before the outbreak of the conflict. This forecast could be revised further downwards if the conflict were to drag on even more.

Inflation set to rise in the coming months

Business confidence indicators also point to a recovery in firms' price expectations across all sectors. The rebound is particularly visible in industry and construction. Nevertheless, expected prices remain well below their 2022 levels, once again illustrating the difference in impact of this energy crisis compared with the previous one. Domestic demand simply appears too weak to trigger price increases similar to those observed at the time.

Moreover, the risk of an inflationary spiral remains more contained in France than in the rest of the euro area. The starting point for inflation is lower than in most European countries. The French economy is characterised by a large share of administered or centrally negotiated prices, which slows the pass-through of cost increases to consumer prices. In addition, the labour market remains fragile. Hiring intentions are weak, and net job creation has been negative for several quarters. Ongoing wage negotiations do not fully incorporate this new inflationary shock. In this context, wage growth is likely to remain more moderate than elsewhere in the euro area, limiting inflationary pressures, especially in services.

This does not, however, imply that inflation will remain under control. Rising energy prices, higher costs for certain key inputs (such as fertilisers or metals), and the rebound in firms’ pricing intentions suggest that French inflation should continue to rise and exceed 2% for several months this year. At this stage, we expect average HICP inflation of 2.1% in 2026, compared with 1.3% in our pre-conflict forecast.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Meta: Golden Buying Opportunity Before Earnings
data.description:
Meta (META) stock has recovered swiftly after quite weak share price performance during the first three months of 2026. The reason why a sharp rebound occurred is because the dip was sentiment-driven, not fundamentals-driven. The share price is currently slightly above the level that was actual when I shared my previous bullish writeup. Meta releases its FQ1 2026 earnings next week, and I think it will be a powerful catalyst that will help support the accelerating share price momentum. Forward multiples look really good, meaning that Meta still remains a compelling investment opportunity.

FQ1 2026 earnings preview

The company's FQ1 2026 earnings are scheduled for April 29, post-market. The last time Meta showed an earnings miss was in FQ4 2022. Since then, we have had 12 earnings seasons, and every time Meta released above-consensus results. On top of that, earnings' beats were really impressive in relative terms, with 11 surprises of 7% and above. What's more, revenue surprises were also impressive and averaged $1+ billion over the last five quarters. Summing it up, Meta's historical earnings discipline makes posting a solid beat on both lines next week highly probable.

Apart from Meta's habit of outperforming on headline numbers, my optimism is also backed by strong revenue and earnings growth momentum demonstrated since the 2022 temporary setback. For FQ1 2026, Wall Street analysts forecast year-over-year revenue growth of 31.3%. This will be a significant growth rate increase compared to the past several quarters. Last time Meta posted a 30%+ year-over-year quarterly revenue growth was in FQ3 2021. The company's bottom line is expected to post a modest 5% year-over-year growth, which is a temporary growth rate decline because of Meta's accelerated investments in AI.

As we all know, the guidance and the management's tone are usually central for any big tech earnings because investing in these stocks is investing in the future. On top of Meta's usual earnings surprise strength and solid momentum from previous earnings, there are a few other reasons supporting confidence. While aggressive investments in AI are likely to keep investors worried, it is important to remember that the technology is already commercialized and Mark Zuckerberg already cited AI-driven revenue gains during Q4 earnings. For this reason, making comparisons with the Metaverse project is not justified.

Beyond that, there were a couple of pieces of encouraging news recently. According to TechCrunch, WhatsApp is testing a premium subscription. Premium features are reported to be similar to the ones offered by Instagram Plus, such as cosmetic customizations, including ringtones, themes, and icons. WhatsApp is already one of Meta's fastest-growing segments, and a likely introduction of a premium subscription is poised to bolster monetization potential of a messenger with 3+ billion active users. Considering the large user base, WhatsApp's expanded monetization potential is likely to positively affect the management's guidance.

While we mentioned that the company already demonstrates AI-driven revenue gains, let's not forget that AI can also be used to help improve cost efficiency. According to Reuters, Meta is reportedly set to cut roughly 10% of its global headcount (approximately 8,000 jobs) as part of a new round of layoffs. This will lead to substantial cost-savings, assuming that the median annual compensation per employee at Meta is approximately $150k. Multiplying the expected headcount cut of 8,000 jobs by the assumed median annual compensation yields a total of $1.2 billion in savings. And the source says that it is the 'first wave of layoffs,' meaning that there might be another round of headcount optimization.

Putting it all together, I think that the stage is set for Meta to post a clean top-and-bottom-line beat supported by the management's optimistic tone during the earnings call.

Valuation analysis

Meta is certainly well-positioned in the AI megatrend, meaning that we should better assess its valuation based on the future earnings growth potential. It follows that sticking to the forward P/E ratio analysis remains a reasonable approach. On top of that, we saw that Meta is historically strong in terms of beating earnings estimates, which is another solid argument speaking for the reliability of the forward P/E approach.

Meta's estimated FY2026 forward P/E ratio is 22.15, which implies a very conservative 1.75% earnings growth this year. Considering the fact that Meta is strong in terms of posting above-consensus results, and already accelerating revenue growth due to AI features, this might be an excessively pessimistic growth assumption. With the double-digit EPS growth expected to resume in FY2027, the forward P/E ratio is poised to drop sharply over the longer-term. In light of this, I believe that Meta is very conservatively valued at the moment. If we take Meta's historical forward P/E ratio of 22.56 and multiply it by Street's FY2027 EPS estimate of 35.86, we get $809 as the estimated fair value per share. This is 20% higher than the current share price.

Wall Street's consensus target price is higher than mine, averaging $855. This is approximately 6% higher compared to my fair value estimate, meaning that analysts likely price an average 6% EPS surprise for FY2027. Taking into account Meta's record of recording confident earnings beats, this assumption doesn't look excessively optimistic.

Putting it all together, Meta's valuation is really attractive at the moment.

Mitigating factors

Sometimes Meta's share price dips after earnings even when the company posts a solid beat on both lines. It might happen even if there are just some slight indications that investments in growth are not paying back as fast as expected. The FQ1 2026 earnings are critical from this perspective because it will be the first quarter after Meta announced a significant boost in AI-related CapEx investment while reporting its FY2025 results. The key question is whether AI-related monetization is able to keep up with the CapEx growth. Even if there is a temporary delay, the market might punish the stock. For example, Microsoft's (MSFT) share price is still yet to recover after the post-earnings sell-off.

From a longer-term perspective, the evolving regulatory environment around social media looks like the primary risk. Several countries have already adopted bans or strict age restrictions on social media for young people, which is a headwind for Meta's core business because the business model depends on the number of users. This factor does not break the bullish thesis for me because while young people are vital in terms of engagement, social media ads monetization still mostly depends on people who already have jobs or businesses. On top of that, Meta has been historically strong in navigating regulatory headwinds and even full platform bans in some countries

Conclusion

Several factors suggest that the stage is set for strong FQ1 2026 earnings. The market might react negatively if Meta once again upgrades its AI CapEx guidance. However, the current nearly 20% discount looks like a golden buying opportunity for long-term investors like me. This is why I am adding more shares and reiterating 'Strong buy' for Meta.

data.title: Bitcoin 2026 Price Prediction: Why The Dollar, Global Liquidity And Volume Signal More Downside Ahead
data.description:
In our last Bitcoin analysis, Bitcoin After the Cycle Peak: What Comes Next and How We're Positioning, we argued that Bitcoin was closer to a cycle low than most believed, even if one final drop remained ahead. Since that publication, the probability of another drop occurring in the coming weeks has increased meaningfully. If it does, it should set up a tradeable bounce within what we believe is an ongoing bear market.

What gives us the confidence that we are in a new bear market cycle, rather than a pullback within a larger uptrend, is that Bitcoin continues to track sentiment patterns and global liquidity cycles with remarkable consistency. Recognizing this unconventional correlation has been the foundation of a framework that has filtered out narrative-driven noise and kept us on the right side of every major Bitcoin trend since 2020. These are themes that we first introduced in August of 2025, when Bitcoin was trading at around $115,000.

Global liquidity appears to be stalling and setting up for a reversal. This is historically not good for Bitcoin and tends to coincide with major tops. This inflection point lines up with our Technical Analysis that has us in the final leg of the multi-year bull market.”

Today, the same sentiment patterns continue to suggest that we are likely going lower, while global liquidity is threatening to get even tighter, not better. Because of this, we continue to maintain a defensive posture regarding Bitcoin until we reach our targets, which we explain in detail in this article as well as our updated game plan.

Why the U.S. Dollar Is the Single Most Important Variable for Bitcoin Prices

By “liquidity”, what we mean is how easily one can access cheap debt. Interestingly, it is not the creation of new debt that dominates capital flows, but the ability to roll over existing obligations. In fact, three out of every four global financial transactions are related to debt refinancing, not expansion. Moreover, nearly 80% of global lending now requires collateral, typically in the form of high-quality, low-volatility assets like U.S. Treasuries.

This creates a framework where liquidity is dictated by how cheaply and easily borrowers can refinance without overcollateralizing. The more capital that’s freed up through this process, the more capital can rotate into risk-on assets such as Bitcoin.

A number of variables influence liquidity conditions. Collectively, these forces determine whether capital and confidence flow into the system or are pulled out:

  • Central bank policy
  • Fiscal spending
  • The Treasury General Account (TGA)
  • Federal Reserve repo operations
  • Broad equity market performance
  • Bond market volatility

However, among all these variables, the most powerful and persistent driver of global liquidity is the strength in the U.S. Dollar.

Roughly 64% of global debt is denominated in USD, which means foreign borrowers who accessed cheap U.S. capital must continue sourcing dollars to service that debt. When the dollar weakens relative to their local currencies, less local currency is needed to meet dollar obligations. This frees up capital that can chase higher-yielding risk assets, including Bitcoin.

It is no coincidence that the 2022 bear market in both Bitcoin and equities bottomed within six weeks of the U.S. Dollar peaking and entering a multi-year downtrend. The inverse correlation between the DXY (the U.S. Dollar Index) and Bitcoin has been remarkably consistent across every major Bitcoin trend cycle.

The chart above illustrates this clearly. When the dollar is rising, Bitcoin tends to experience heightened volatility and price pressure. When the dollar is in a larger decline, it has historically coincided with Bitcoin's major bull cycles.

After a 3.5-year drawdown, the evidence suggests the dollar's bear market has ended and a new uptrend is underway.

The above chart shows that the 2022 peak and bear market that followed has taken the form of a large corrective (A,B,C) pattern that has just been completed.

From the 2022 top, we have an initial drop into July 2023 (A Wave), followed by an overlapping bounce into January 2025 that failed to make a new high (B Wave). Finally, an aggressive, near-vertical decline that completed the final swing of this pattern (C Wave). Within the C Wave, the momentum indicator (MACD) reached its most extreme reading, characteristic of a third wave, before registering two higher lows against lower price lows, a classic fifth-wave signature.

Confirmation that the dollar's bear market concluded in March 2026 came when DXY posted its first higher high since the C Wave began. It is now working on its first higher low. A sustained break above $100.60 in the coming weeks would guarantee that a new dollar uptrend is in place.

The impact on Bitcoin is already visible. DXY completed its bear market pattern on September 17, 2025. Bitcoin topped near $126,000 on October 6th, just 19 days later. Since then, Bitcoin has declined over 52%, confirming that the inverse correlation between DXY and Bitcoin remains firmly intact.

Should DXY break above $100.60 as the pattern suggests, it would signal an extended dollar uptrend ahead, further tightening global liquidity and continuing to be a meaningful headwind for Bitcoin and risk assets broadly.

Bitcoin Volume Analysis: A Bear Market Signal That Has Worked Since 2022

In our November 2025 report, we flagged a concerning shift in Bitcoin's buying patterns that was inconsistent with a healthy bull market. From the 2022 low onward, volume had been expanding as price rose - a sign that increasing buyer conviction was supporting the uptrend. Corrections in Bitcoin during this period tracked declining volume, meaning fewer sellers were showing up on pullbacks. This is a textbook characteristic of strong and healthy uptrends, illustrated by the green shading in the chart above.

That pattern broke down around March 2025. As Bitcoin made its final run to all-time highs, volume declined rather than expanded, which was the first time this had occurred since the 2022 low. That divergence was a significant warning, and in hindsight, signaling an early warning that volume was rejecting the move into the October top.

Volume has since told the opposite story. As shown in the red shading, it is now expanding as price falls and contracting as Bitcoin bounces. This is a textbook distribution pattern that signals a clear and meaningful shift in investor convictions.

Today, Bitcoin is in another overlapping bounce on decelerating volume, while the RSI (momentum indicator) is back within the range where bear market rallies have historically exhausted themselves. The setup is consistent with another leg lower ahead.

The developing pattern bears a notable resemblance to 2022, which featured a series of sharp vertical drops interrupted by weak, overlapping bounces — a complex corrective structure that took months to resolve. The current price action in 2026 appears to be forming a similar pattern.

The key levels to watch are straightforward. A break below $62,534 would likely trigger a continuation toward the $55,000–$40,000 range, with the $48,000–$46,000 zone representing our highest-probability target, and the region where a more meaningful low can develop.

Conversely, if Bitcoin holds $62,534 and rallies above $79,125, we will consider the larger bounce scenario in play, though we would expect that rally to ultimately fail below the $106,000–$116,000 resistance zone.

Why We Remain Long-Term Bitcoin Bulls Despite Staying Defensively Positioned Today

In conclusion, we sold over 80% of our crypto exposure between November 2024 - October 2025, locking in meaningful gains before the major decline unfolded. Today, we remain defensively positioned for continued volatility over the coming weeks to months. Whether this volatility resolves on the next drop or extends into 2027 and takes prices lower than most expect, our long-term outlook on Bitcoin remains firmly bullish, and the reasons are rooted not in price charts, but in the structural forces reshaping the global financial system.

It is worth stepping back to appreciate how far Bitcoin's credibility has come. The same established investors and institutions that once dismissed it as a fraud have not only reversed course; they have embedded it into the global financial system. BlackRock, Fidelity, JPMorgan, and the U.S. government itself now hold or facilitate Bitcoin exposure. This is not speculation. It is the current reality. And it did not happen by accident.

Bitcoin's ability to generally function as gold, without the storage, custody, or geographical constraints, makes it a uniquely compelling store of value in a world carrying 236% total Debt/GDP and no credible path to reducing it.

The numbers are sobering. The U.S. alone sits at 121% Debt/GDP, with 31% of all tax receipts now consumed by debt service alone. For the first time in recorded history, America spends more servicing its debt than funding its military. Historian Niall Ferguson, through his extensive studies of fallen empires, identified precisely this crossover as the inflection point marking the beginning of terminal decline for empires, without a single historical exception.

What makes this more alarming is that there is no relief in sight. The U.S. is projected to run a 5.8% of GDP deficit this year, averaging 6.1% over the next decade. The trajectory is not stabilizing; it is accelerating.

Governments in this position have three options: (1) They can grow GDP faster than debt, but with debt expanding at roughly 6% annually, sustaining that pace of nominal growth is not realistic for a mature economy. (2) They can raise taxes. However, closing a $1.9 trillion deficit gap through taxation alone would require a 34% increase in tax receipts, a figure that would almost certainly cross the threshold of diminishing returns and slow the very growth needed to service the debt. That leaves the third option, and the one most governments with reserve currency status have chosen throughout history without fail. (3) print money to cover the bills and pass the cost to citizens through inflation. The bill is always paid, just not in the way most people recognize it.

This is where Bitcoin becomes not merely interesting, but structurally important. Unlike the U.S. dollar, which must expand by roughly 6% annually just to cover the deficit, Bitcoin cannot be inflated. Its supply is relatively fixed, and its scarcity is absolute. More importantly, it is increasingly recognized, regardless of whether one agrees with it, as a store of value that crosses borders and transfers directly between parties without intermediaries or the permission of any government.

In a world where more currency must be created to fund ever-growing government spending, and where the political will to stop does not exist, an asset that is widely considered valuable and remains largely fixed in supply becomes, by definition, more valuable over time. This is not a narrative, but simple arithmetic.

This was Bitcoin's original purpose, as laid out in its founding white paper. The more acute the problem becomes, the more compelling Bitcoin becomes as an alternative to a centralized fiat system that is, by its own projections, borrowing its way toward an unavoidable reckoning.

However, unlike many, our system has, so far, helped us lock in gains from epic bull market runs, while side stepping the devastating drops that follow. We remain defensive over the intermediate term basis, as we patiently wait for the next best long-term buying opportunity to present itself.

Please note: The Tech Insider Network conducts research and draws conclusions for the Fund’s positions. We then share that information with our readers. This is not a guarantee of a stock’s performance. Please consult your personal financial advisor before buying any stock in the companies mentioned in this analysis.

data.title: The Eurozone's Goods Trade Surplus Is Under Structural Threat
data.description:
By Bert Colijn, Chief Economist, Netherlands, and Amrita Naik Nimbalkar, Economist, Global Macro

The eurozone growth model, built on competitive exports of goods and access to cheap inputs, is under structural pressure. The energy price shocks, increased Chinese competitiveness, and the changing role of the US in the world economy work against the exceptionally high trade surplus that the eurozone has had since the global financial crisis.

The Middle East war is likely to weaken the trade balance in the short run. But the problem is also a structural one, as European companies are facing stronger global competition, particularly from China, which has made rapid advances in several key sectors. Export growth is therefore becoming a less important engine of the economy, which affects the growth outlook.

How the eurozone growth model is being reshaped

The eurozone's economic model was reshaped after the global financial crisis of 2008 and the subsequent euro crisis. Pre-2008, Germany ran large trade surpluses, which were counterbalanced by the trade deficits of countries in the south. When the euro crisis forced southern European countries to tighten their belts, their trade deficits disappeared. The policy response to the euro crisis always implicitly aimed to impose the German economic model on the rest of Europe. And at least for a while, it succeeded. Most of the eurozone effectively became more "German"; i.e., wage growth was more subdued, imports were modest and exports strengthened. This resulted in a trade surplus of around €50bn per quarter for the eurozone as a whole in the late 2010s.

But this decade, the goods surplus has come under pressure. This is mainly due to more expensive energy, but the eurozone's competitive position has also weakened due to higher wages and increased external competition. Services trade has been less affected so far, but the impact on goods trade can be significant. For the years ahead, we see more downside for the goods trade balance as increased defence spending will likely boost imports, the competitive position of China continues to show strength, and energy dependence remains a weak spot for the eurozone.

The eurozone goods trade balance is structurally lower since the pandemic

The war in Iran adds renewed pressures on the eurozone trade balance

That steady, strong trade surplus of the 2010s was interrupted abruptly by the energy shock of 2021/22. While the eurozone had always been a net importer of energy, the dramatic reduction of Russian gas supply pushed energy costs sharply higher, dragging the total trade balance into a deficit. Since then, it has diversified away from cheap Russian energy and towards pricier alternatives, which has kept pressure on the trade balance. Overall, the eurozone goods trade surplus still remains almost 30% lower than in 2019.

Renewed tensions linked to the oil shortages arising from the war in the Middle East will put the eurozone’s trade surplus under pressure again this year. To illustrate this, let's assume that goods trade volumes remain unchanged from 2025. Focusing only on the first-round effects of higher energy import prices, the trade balance could fall sharply to €49bn in 2026 from €149bn in 2025. This scenario assumes our base case, in which disruptions at the Strait of Hormuz continue until July but ease gradually.

In a more pessimistic scenario where the disruptions are stronger and continue until August, the eurozone's trade balance risks falling into a significant deficit again at -€115bn in 2026.

Clearly, the eurozone’s dependence on external energy makes it vulnerable to short- and long-term shocks in the energy markets. After a period of very benign geopolitics, the renewed tensions of the 2020s are increasing volatility in the eurozone’s trade balance and, for now, are resulting in a lower average trade surplus.

To President Trump’s delight, the trade surplus with the US is also coming down

Eurozone exports to the US, its top export destination, surged ahead of President Trump’s "Liberation Day" announcement in April 2025. As US firms rushed to frontload goods to avoid elevated tariffs, exports to the US jumped by over 60% year-on-year in March 2025. Medicine and pharmaceuticals made up over 40% of total shipments that month. This was short-lived. In the second half of the year, exports to the US fell by 20% (almost €60bn) compared to the first half, leading to a smaller trade surplus with the US. While some of this lost trade was diverted elsewhere, it was not enough to fully offset the decline.

We are wary about drawing meaningful conclusions on the back of the tariff developments just yet. As things settle, we will see just how much export volume to the US has been permanently lost. We don't expect the impact to be insignificant, but neither do we expect it to be dramatic. At the same time though, energy imports from the US have increased as the eurozone has reduced its reliance on Russia for gas. And the European Commission has promised a lot more imports to come in the years ahead. With more US LNG export capacity coming on-line, this adds to the changing trade relationship between the US and eurozone. However, if overall demand for LNG remains the same, these additional US supplies would largely substitute for imports from other countries such as Norway and Qatar.

Yet, the eurozone’s trade rebalancing is increasingly a China story

Energy cost pressures and tariff frictions are creating short-term stress for eurozone trade. But when it comes to the imbalance with its main trading partners, China stands out.

Increased Chinese competition in the eurozone and in third markets has been a trend that restarted with the Covid pandemic, and has been intensifying as Chinese production has moved up the value chain. At the same time, weak domestic demand in China has added to overcapacity, which has contributed to a more aggressive export strategy. We have written extensively about this here.

China has been gaining global share in some high-value sectors

The eurozone has also become more reliant on China over recent years. The share of imported goods from China has risen from 14% to 16% between 2019 and 2025. The growth in imports has been staggering, at 51% over the same period, while exports to China declined by 1%. As a result, the eurozone’s goods trade deficit with China now stands at €156bn more than in 2019.

Eurozone goods trade balance with China is structurally lower since pre-pandemic

The eurozone’s core export industries now face strong Chinese competition

What makes this more concerning is where the pressure is coming from. China’s surge in competitiveness is most apparent in sectors that are the eurozone’s core export sectors: machinery, transport equipment, chemicals and pharmaceuticals. These industries together made up almost 70% of total eurozone exports in 2025. Here, higher energy costs have already eroded competitiveness, especially in Germany.

The strain is most visible in machinery and transport equipment, the eurozone’s largest export category (50% of exports in 2025). Despite increased exports to the US, Turkey and the UK since 2019, the sector’s surplus has shrunk by over €70bn, and much of that deterioration reflects trade with China. The eurozone has moved from being a net exporter of road vehicles to China to a net importer, driven largely by China’s dominance in electric vehicles. Imports of electrical machinery from China have also surged, while eurozone exports have barely moved.

Pharmaceuticals have been a bright spot in eurozone trade. Exports in 2025 were over 80% higher than in 2019, particularly to the US, which were over 150% higher. Imports have increased too but remain lower in value terms overall. Even here, however, Chinese competition is gaining ground. The share of eurozone pharmaceutical imports coming from China has more than doubled from 3% to 8%. Concerns are more pronounced in the chemical sector. Here, the share of eurozone imports of organic chemicals from China surged from 14% to 30% between 2019 and 2025. With the domestic sector suffering from high energy prices, Chinese competition is becoming a structural threat.

Eurozone imports from China are rising in sectors critical to exports

This contributes to a weakening of export growth and downbeat exporters

The mood among eurozone exporters has therefore soured in recent years. With increased competition in the global market, the eurozone export growth model has become harder to sustain. This is not a phenomenon specific to 2025, but a trend that has been building for some time. Businesses’ perceptions of competitiveness inside and outside the eurozone have historically moved in tandem over time but have seen a clear decoupling since 2022. While firms were already negative about their competitive position within the eurozone, perceptions of competitiveness outside the eurozone have since fallen to all-time lows.

This weakening is also visible in the gross export data. Since 1996, exports have typically outpaced GDP growth during expansions but have also contracted more sharply during downturns. So, it is striking that in the current period of expansion, export growth has failed to outpace GDP growth. This suggests that the contribution of exports to overall growth has been steadily diminishing.

The more competitive global environment is translating into weaker eurozone competitiveness

Expect a more volatile trade balance with a structurally lower surplus for goods

The benign days of the 2010s, from which the eurozone has profited so much, are over. The rockier 2020s are not beneficial for a trade bloc reliant on others for key inputs and facing more fierce competition.

Looking ahead, the eurozone’s goods trade story goes beyond the short-term dynamics. Energy shocks and tariffs can have transitory impacts on the trade balance. Trade deals with other countries - think of Mercosur, India and Australia - can increase trade flows but with an uncertain impact on the trade balance.

Also, the trade surplus is feeling pressure from the import side. Stronger domestic demand, fuelled by fiscal stimulus, is widely expected to modestly add to growth. This mainly comes from increasing defence spending, which will heavily rely on imports (at least in the early years of increased spending).

But the bigger structural risk is stronger competition from China, which has intensified this decade and is still resulting in increased imports from the eurozone and more limited export opportunities for the eurozone’s largest export sectors. Unless the eurozone regains competitiveness in its core industries, which is not easy to achieve, the days of a strong goods trade surplus supporting economic growth could well be over. While stronger domestic demand can be positive, this seems to be driven more by weakness than strength.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Cytokinetics: Strong Story, But Valuation Leaves Limited Upside
data.description:
Introduction

Cytokinetics (CYTK) has crossed into the commercial stage with aficamten’s (branded MYQORZO) approval in oHCM. The product was launched in January, and the first real revenue quarter is the one ongoing now, with earnings released early next month. The company didn’t mention revenue or prescription or patient numbers in the Barclays Conference, either, so next month is the first glimpse we will have at those critical commercial numbers.

Yet the market is already valuing CYTK as a proven commercial company. EV/Sales is nearly ~100x, even though they barely have any sales. That one number tells it all. CYTK is already priced for success—at ~$66 per share and roughly 123M shares outstanding, CYTK’s equity value is about ~$8.1B. That isn’t a bad thing, but it does make new investors want to ask, What's left for latecomers?

To answer that, the analysis must be grounded in a bottom-up valuation of aficamten across its approved and potential indications.

What Are We Valuing?

There are three elements to evaluate here. This constitutes the company pipeline, shown below:

The first and most important is the currently approved indication. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is of two types, obstructive (oHCM) or non-obstructive (nHCM). Current approval is in oHCM, and nHCM is the next pipeline target via the ACACIA phase 3 trial. These two are the current and secondary upside factors for CYTK. A third bucket includes the molecules omecamtiv mecarbil and ulacamten. These target HFrEF and HFpEF, respectively, in mid-to-late stage trials. All indications are heart diseases, and each molecule is a cardiac myosin inhibitor—or activator, in omecamtiv’s case.

Now, as to the target diseases, in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), the heart muscles become too weak and cannot pump effectively, while in heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), almost the opposite thing happens, where the heart contracts normally but remains stiff and cannot fill properly. Ejection fraction is a measure of how much blood the left ventricle can pump. So in HFrEF, EF is reduced, while in HFpEF, the ejection fraction is preserved. Omecamtiv is a cardiac myosin activator, helping the heart muscles contract better in HFrEF, while ulacamten is a cardiac myosin inhibitor, and targets a subset of HFpEF where too much contractility may be causing the disease.

So to answer the original question, total valuation is:

Total Equity Value = oHCM Value + nHCM Value (risk-adjusted) + Pipeline Optionality

oHCM: From Patient Pool to Revenue

Patient Funnel

Cytokinetics frames the oHCM opportunity as more than 100,000 patients eligible for cardiac myosin inhibitor therapy. This is a useful place to start, but it is not exactly the actual number of patients that will be treated by aficamten. For that, we will have to apply penetration assumptions.

So, for example:

  • At 10% penetration, treated patients = 100,000 × 10% = 10,000

  • At 15% penetration, treated patients = 100,000 × 15% = 15,000

  • At 20% penetration, treated patients = 100,000 × 20% = 20,000

This range is more realistic, as it is derived from our estimate of the interplay between competition, physician behavior, and payer dynamics.

Revenue Per Patient

Cytokinetics itself says the market is $10B across 100k patents. That gives us a rough $100,000 revenue per patient annually. However, this figure is likely a gross pricing estimate. We must account for rebates, discounts, and so on—so net revenue per patient will likely be in the $80,000 to $90,000 range.

Now we can use that range to convert treated patients into revenue.

Revenue Build

Applying both penetration and net pricing assumptions, my conservative scenario, with 10,000 treated patients at $80,000 annually, total revenue would be $800M.

In a base case scenario, with 15,000 patients and $85,000 pricing, total revenue would be $1.275B.

A more optimistic scenario with 20,000 patients and $90,000 pricing gets us to $1.8B in total revenue.

These three cases define a realistic peak revenue range for oHCM.

Royalty Adjustment

Now, Cytokinetics is paying Royalty Pharma approximately 4.5% of net sales as a royalty deal.

Applying this adjustment:

  • Bear case: $800M × (1 – 0.045) ≈ $764M

  • Base case: $1.275B × (1 – 0.045) ≈ $1.22B

  • Bull case: $1.8B × (1 – 0.045) ≈ $1.72B

We will use these adjusted figures for valuation.

Valuation Multiple

I am going to apply a sales multiple and not an earnings multiple since CYTK is still launching and is not yet profitable. Given the competition from already-entrenched Camzyos, we should use a 3-5x sales multiple. Using 4x as a midpoint:

  • Bear case: $764M × 4 = ~$3.1B

  • Base case: $1.22B × 4 = ~$4.9B

  • Bull case: $1.72B × 4 = ~$6.9B

Interpretation

We can see this right away: even under the most favorable assumptions, oHCM does not support the entire current market cap. At most, it supports ~40% to ~80% of the value. The remainder must come from another source—primarily nHCM.

Competition: Why Penetration Is Capped

My lower penetration assumptions can be questioned; however, the competition from Camzyos compels me to keep it capped (some pipeline candidates exist, but most are myosin inhibitors like these two). Physicians typically do not like to move away from entrenched drugs that are working—even if not perfectly. Cytokinetics itself says switching existing patients is not their primary strategy—even though they do claim better efficacy and safety for their molecule.

Instead, they expect to see growth from new patients—here, it may be easier to convince a physician to prescribe a newer drug with better trial data. The problem is, even if CYTK manages to capture over 50% of new patients, that still does not make them the dominant player. The entrenched base still exists. Since treatment is lifetime, that base stays intact for years.

This is why I have bounded the penetration range to 10 and 20%. More bullish narratives will reach for 30 or even 50%—but that’s really reaching.

nHCM: The Primary Upside Lever

Strategic Importance

nHCM may potentially be a larger opportunity than oHCM, primarily because there are currently no approved therapies. Camzyos failed a phase 3 trial in this indication. If ACACIA, toplining in Q2, is successful, and aficamten ultimately gets approved, it will be the only kid on the block.

Bottom-Up Modeling Approach

However, at the current stage of its lifecycle, aficamten in nHCM should not be modeled to mirror oHCM. Management does note that the patient population is roughly similar in size. But the commercial case is different. First, nHCM does not present clinical gradations that can guide treatment, so dosing is less intuitive, and finally, we have no idea how payers will behave. So the effective treatment population is smaller, and adoption is going to be slower as well. nHCM, we must remember, does not have any approved treatment, so CYTK will be building the market it is selling into.

So we start with the same number as oHCM:

  • Eligible population = ~100,000 patients

But due to the technical/diagnostic issues I discussed, I will apply a discount to get the actual number. So:

  • Treatable pool ≈ 100,000 × 65% = 65,000 patients

Now we come to penetration assumptions. In oHCM, the drug was new, but the mechanism was proven in the indication, so we took a higher penetration rate. Here, it will be completely unproven in the real world. So our penetration assumptions are more conservative:

  • Bear: 5%

  • Base: 10%

  • Bull: 15%

This yields treated patients:

  • Bear: 65,000 × 5% = 3,250 patients

  • Base: 65,000 × 10% = 6,500 patients

  • Bull: 65,000 × 15% = 9,750 patients

Revenue Build

Here, we use the same net pricing plan as oHCM (~$85,000 per patient annually):

  • Bear: 3,250 × $85,000 = $276M

  • Base: 6,500 × $85,000 = $552M

  • Bull: 9,750 × $85,000 = $829M

Applying the 4.5% Royalty Pharma burden:

  • Bear: $276M × 0.955 ≈ $264M

  • Base: $552M × 0.955 ≈ $527M

  • Bull: $829M × 0.955 ≈ $792M

Risk Adjustment and Time Discounting

Now, after building revenue, we will apply the probability of success. We will use a 50% success rate, conservative for a phase 3 stage molecule, but reasonable since camzyos with the same mechanism failed the nHCM indication. We will use a time discount factor of 0.7.

Now, risk-adjusted revenue becomes:

  • Bear: $264M × 0.5 × 0.7 ≈ $92M

  • Base: $527M × 0.5 × 0.7 ≈ $185M

  • Bull: $792M × 0.5 × 0.7 ≈ $277M

Applying a 4x sales multiple:

  • Bear: $92M × 4 ≈ $370M

  • Base: $185M × 4 ≈ $740M

  • Bull: $277M × 4 ≈ $1.1B

So the range we get for nHCM is $0.4B–$1.1B (base ≈ $0.7B). This may look lower than typical top-down estimates that assume a huge billion-plus sales figure and start from there. However, this is more sober, and it immediately tells us the market is implicitly pricing nHCM closer to the upper end of the outcome, rather than the base case.

Rest of the pipeline

CYTK has two other assets. Now, omecamtiv mecarbil has failed in a broader set of HF and is now targeting a sicker subset. Unlike aficamten, it is an activator - completely different mechanism. Used to be partnered with Amgen, but Amgen walked away after seeing GALACTIC data. So this is a distressed asset they are trying to repurpose—hardly commands the same type of value as aficamten.

Ulacamten has the same mechanism but in an earlier stage.

Taking both together, I will give a valuation range of $500M to $1B, risk-adjusted and time discounted. The base case would be ~$700M.

SOTP Valuation

Putting all the components together, we have an oHCM base case at ~$4.9B, nHCM risk-adjusted value is ~0.7B at base, and the pipeline we are assigning a value of ~0.7B. Total equity value comes to ~$6.3B. That gives me a per share value of ~$51. Compared to current prices, my base case is a 20-25% downside.

Timeline

The revenue figures I have discussed here do not all materialize at once, or even in the short term. Aficamten was launched in early 2026, and the initial ramp depends on physician education, REMS onboarding, and so on. This will take time. Peak revenue is unlikely to be achieved in less than 3-5 years’ time. So we will be discounting future revenues for the delay. This is even more true of the nHCM indication, which will need another 1.5-2 years for approval, and peak revenue may take another 3-5 years from there.

Sensitivity: What Needs to Go Right

Bull Case

For that gap to close, two things must work simultaneously. First, oHCM penetration must reach ~20%. Second, nHCM adoption must be stronger than the base case. That will give us:

  • oHCM ≈ ~$6.9B

  • nHCM ≈ ~$1.1B

  • Pipeline ≈ ~$0.8B

Total = ~$8.8–9.0B per share = ~$72–75, a ~12–15% upside.

Bear Case

On the other hand, if execution is weaker than my base case, oHCM penetration is only ~10%, and nHCM fails. That will give us:

  • oHCM ≈ ~$3.1B

  • nHCM ≈ $0

  • Pipeline ≈ ~$0.5B

Total ≈ ~$3.6 per share ≈ ~$30, a ~50+% discount from current prices.

Insider sales

There has been considerable insider selling in CYTK over the last couple of years. Here’s some data:

The red rows are outright insider sellings, while the yellow ones are option exercises. While option exercises are simply predetermined sales of options given to insiders, the outright sales are not so positive, and often indicate management/insider lack of faith in their own business. This is not always true—sometimes, purely a sudden need for cash may lead to a sale—however, so many sales from so many different insiders only bolster my case—that CYTK’s success has been priced in already.

Conclusion: A Prove-It Valuation

Cytokinetics is no longer an early-stage biotech that is being mispriced. It is now being paid premium multiples to execute well—possibly too well for a first time commercial stage company. The expectations are not unrealistic, but they are high enough that CYTK doesn’t get to make a mistake and say, “Sorry.” If it fails to execute any part of its various segments—including the pipeline—the valuation is going to look expensive.

On the other hand, oHCM does largely explain current value—even though the estimates are bullish. nHCM, when modeled rigorously like here, may contribute less than the bullish models—but it still does have substantial secondary value, and may pop if results become progressively convincing. The pipeline has been conservatively valued, and here, too, if things turn out well, there’s going to be decent upside.

Right now, CYTK is a “prove-it” story, and the market is already assuming it will prove it. That makes me more cautious, and I will remain on the sidelines here.
